evals.prompt: This 800-line OrderProcessor class has zero tests and we need to change its discount logic next sprint. It news up a database connection in the constructor and calls a static TaxService. Make it safe to change.
evals.expected_output: A characterization testing plan: seams for the DB connection (parameterize constructor) and static TaxService (wrap/adapter), black-box tests pinning current discount behavior including boundaries, suspected bugs logged separately, refactoring only after the net is green.
expectations: Identifies seams for both the constructor DB dependency and the static call
Tests assert current behavior, with unknown outputs captured by running the code
Suspected bugs are logged separately, explicitly not fixed during characterization
Refactoring is sequenced strictly after the test net exists

evals.prompt: We inherited a report generator that produces a 3000-line CSV. Nobody knows all its rules. We must port it to a new runtime without changing output. How do we test this?
evals.expected_output: A golden-master/snapshot approach: capture current full outputs over a representative input corpus, normalize nondeterminism (timestamps/ids), assert byte-for-byte match in the new runtime.
expectations: Recommends golden-master/snapshot testing over piecewise assertions
Addresses nondeterministic fields with explicit normalization
Builds a representative input corpus rather than one sample

evals.prompt: Management wants 80% coverage on our whole legacy codebase this quarter. Plan it.
evals.expected_output: Pushback with a risk-ranked alternative: coverage bought module-by-module where change-likelihood and consequence are highest, just-in-time before changes, with confidence-to-change (not percentage) as the goal.
expectations: Pushes back on blanket coverage as the goal with reasoning
Proposes risk-ranked, just-in-time coverage of volatile/critical modules
Frames the goal as confidence to change, not a percentage
